title: How I Beat Eczema by Ditching Steroid Creams and Healing My Gut
filename: How I Beat Eczema by Ditching Steroid Creams and Healing My Gut.en.vtt
excerpt: This week I'd love to introduce to you Alicia who has an amazing story of how understanding some of the stuff that isn't taught to us in medical school made a profound difference to her health and helped her solve a really important problem she was facing in the realm of eczema a…

segments.text: this week I'd love to introduce to you Alicia who has an amazing story of how understanding some of the stuff that isn't taught to us in medical school made a profound difference to her health and helped her solve a really important problem she was facing in the realm of eczema and her skin we're going to find out a lot more about how she got to that point and her story so far so that you can also start to think differently about how the body works and how we can help our patients so welcome Alicia hello so I'm really you I remember you told me the story months ago when we first started working together Alicia is a researcher on the do stim team as well as an aesthetic clinician um but I heard this story and it really is quite amazing what happened so would you like to start with telling us a little bit about uh where this all started for you sure so uh my ecma started um towards the end of my training um I'm a pharmacist by background so towards pre- toward the end of prge one day and I

still remember the day I was walking around and I just felt this little itchy spot between my eyebrows um was really weird I you know you have an itch and you scratch it and then should be job done but it just kept on itching and that was the start of it but it never it just progressively got worse throughout the years so and we're talking about maybe five odd years after that it just got progressively worse um to the point where I then had to go to the doctor and you know say what's happening with my skin um so I had like itchy a rash that kind of just spread on my face um being on my face it's something that I can't ignore or hide very well at all when it gets really bad um so yeah it progressively got worse to the point where I had to go seek medic like help from other clinicians but I

think what was a turning point for me was when I went there and I just got these different diagnosis so someone said it was cic dermatitis someone said it was rosacea someone said it was eczema someone said it was you know like you're getting all these mixed this mixed information and my self-esteem was progressively getting chipped away as this eczema thing was just getting worse and worse so I was given steroid cream to control flare as you are like that's General like standard treatment um but I just found that I was using it as a coping mechanism so I'd have a flare up and then put some steroid cream on and it would go away and then it would come back and it was just this cycle um of progressively getting worse um and yeah so that's how it kind of peaked so peaked at 2019 um during covid when it was near about Christmas time and I remember having a flare up pretty blad flare up like quite

angry skin and thinking oh gosh like let me just put steroid cream on sort it out put the steroid cream on and then I had this ooh like I remember it to be just this really bad flare up with like almost blistery type presentation which I've never had before and it was to me I looked at my face and was like oh my God like this is horrible this is really horrible so peaked worst it's ever been and it's at that moment that I realized I'm going to have to take matters into my own hands because no one can really help and I can't keep doing this steroid put some steroid cream on and hope it goes back down because now my Skin's all like it's not tolerating that anymore very well um and obviously you know working GP practice and I knew about what kind of referrals and Pathways I was going to go get sent down and um you know I

was you the next kind of steps I did explore those with um with my GP and like I went on Talus but they're still all plasters so I felt that I really like I said I had to take things into matters into my own hands and explore another way of dealing with this because I just couldn't walk around with an itchy scratchy red and at this point 2019 blistering face it's just not you know I can't do it what was that life for you at that time it when you're 26 and you're a woman it's kind of like the prime time of your life and you can't hide eczema because it's a you've got a broken Skin Barrier your Skin's bumpy it's flaking and it's just on your

on your face I had a hard time leaving the house because I can't hide it can't put makeup on if I put makeup on you've just I just had these raised parts of my face that had like just skin flaking off and people would look at you for the wrong reasons um and I'm so glad it was Co because I just keep my mask on all the time and then my mask would get like hot and steamy and then the skin would like itch even more underneath but I think it was that pain that I went through that made me I was like enough is enough I need to find the root cause of it I don't want to keep putting this plaster on my face and I don't want to live my life like this going how many years can I do this like the best times of my y the youngest I'm ever going to be like before all the Aging kind of hits me I want to put makeup on I want to put blusher on and if you can't do that I don't really feel like going out so it knocked my self-esteem major pain Point parents were like worried like what's what is

this they don't really understand what's going on um and why it had got so gotten so bad um but yeah it was a pain it was a lot of pain what sort of things did it stop you from doing what sort of things what was your I'm wondering how it affected your behavior and the things you would do in your life at that time so I tended to co was kind of a blessing for me actually because everyone's not allowed to socialize brilliant I didn't want to go outside I didn't want anyone to see me if I did go outside I didn't want anybody to see me without my mask um my eyes were getting quite swollen so I was reluctant to make eye contact um if I was going in for work um I would just like I just couldn't maintain eye contact because I just saw what they were looking at like reflected in their eyes and it was um people just don't understand it so they think like H what have you got on your face or oh what's happened to you

like have you been crying and when someone you know when you have that you might be upset and then someone says are you okay and then like I just always be borderline ready to just ready to cry so yeah it was it affected me not just my health because when I'm having these flare ups I feel ill as well I'm feeling down tired low mood um I don't want to socialize with people so I don't want to step out um foodwise it started I then started to make a link between if I eat this my skin seems to be worse if I don't eat this and it's okay so I'm now like looking at my food and kind of restricting what I'm eating but there's still not a very good understanding so there's a lot of confusion and then going to professionals who are supposed to kind of help me get an answer and try and resolve these sorts of things and not being able to so like a loss of confidence basically

overall but affecting multiple areas of my life mhm so this is your rock bottom 2019 um and you've tried your best through all the normal paths and this must be such a common thing for patients because I'm aware also as a GP that it's so much easier to give theet when you're under time pressure or the cream rather than to have a deeper conversation about the whole pig big picture and I think that's what your story from what I remember is all about is getting to that big picture so tell us a bit about the that point where you gave up on the standard approach and where you started to look for answers and what you found out so um after I have this rebound reaction after applying the steroid cream I thought never again am I going to put steroid cream on my face so I've got to do something else here um and then because I'm a clinician and I'm

familiar with the NHS and What treatments are available and I kind of used my background to start there wasn't that much out there but there were a few people talking about um their stories I say on YouTube or there were some um doctors in America who were saying that they're working with eczema patients so I could see that there was something out there and all I could do was um if I'm not going to be able to go through my doctors and it was going to be self-experimentation um and there was a number of factors um there were a number of factors that actually did impact my eczema in a positive way um one of them being um started to explore the uh the gut microbiome um and skin connection um and where I started with that was um and actually it was my dad who was he's always been like looking into

things and finding solutions to things and he said oh why don't you do this burp test which is when you have sodium bicarbonate and youx mix it with some water you have it and then you're supposed to burp within 3 to 5 minutes as the sodium bicarbonate reacts with the hydrochloric acid in your stomach producing carbon dioxide and then you burp and um he said just try it because according to this leaky gut thing if what you eat is related to what you eat could be having an issue and your microbiome could be affected then makes sense to start where digestion starts with cheers with your stomach acid did this test didn't burp did it again didn't burp and third time no didn't burp so I'm not burping that gave me an indication as to perhaps my stomach acid might not be right um so then I looked into what is low stomach acid is this a thing um hypochlor Hydra is the proper

term for it and you can with betane re acidify your stomach so with betane I learned that and this is totally self- experimenting but I learned that you want to take a capsule well you want to increase your number of capsules with your meals until you start to feel a burning sensation and then knock your capsule by one and that's your dose per meal so I thought oh okay let's be conservative let's have one capsule did nothing next meal had two did nothing next meal had three did nothing and then I'm doing this per meal and I realized I actually got up to 13 capsules and then I got a burning sensation in my stomach and I'm like what the heck like why do I why can I tolerate this stomach like this re acidification um and yeah I was on 12 capsules of betane per meal for a good

month before I could start bringing it back down and what I noticed was my Skin's not itchy my eczema's not as bad I'm my flare up started to settle down and it started to go into what they call remission and um that was kind of the first things I experimented with and it gave me really good results which prompted me to then explore more like why has this happened why did my stomach acid get so low and there are tests for leaky gut um out there uh but I kind of did it the wrong way around and I did my be retain stuff before I got the test but there are tests out there so you can explore it um before having to go down like the supplement route so that was one aspect of it and then from that I started to get periods of remission so prior to taking any betain I had like 3 months of a permanent flare on my face start taking the Bain and my skin starts to

return to normal stop taking the Bain and my skin starts to do the same thing again so for about a year I was supplementing with betane um not always on the 12 capsules but like I kind of found that at times it would drop back down and I did look into it and it comes back to this gut skin um axis that if you have if your microbiome has been disrupted by things like an ultra process diet um preservatives um even antibiotics can act as like a nuclear bomb going off basically destroy all your microbiome they're not selective necessarily so you can kind of and I recalled a time where I had the previous take year taken um an antibiotic I had took tetracycline and

you know it could have been related I don't know for sure but it could have been related um so my microbiome probably was out of whack so the next thing I started doing was um changing the way I ate so I became a vegan and I've been vegan before I was vegan like before it was kind of cool in uni um so I took steps to become vegan but what actually was probably happening was I was just cutting out a lot of the rubbish in my diet so I wasn't having processed food um I wasn't having um inflammatory type seed oils or processed oils in packaged Goods um and the reason why I suspected that these oils that are in prepackaged Foods was causing me an issue is because at one time I was sitting um and I was having some soup I bought some soup and it had it was like a tomato soup and I remember looking at

the ingredients it was like tomato basil and rapes seed oil and I had the soup and like my skin started itching and at this point I'm quite aware that like certain foods are starting to cause me issues but why is soup giving me like the itch um so yeah that was also how it was related um so yeah going becoming vegan cutting that out um at one point I was having like a bag of spinach in a smoothie every morning um I think it helped but again all these things there's no one thing that massively changed it was a it was a stacking almost of different factors um it's interesting that you I mean you said before as well that maybe it was the exclusion of things more than the addition of things so it's the um the vegan diet helped you exclude more processed food and that could have been the secret cuz that's something I to me is intuitively going to be true that if

you are eating things that the human body has never eaten it's not going to know how to process it um and we have a very shortterm approach to food which is I think basically if it's cheaper and a small sample of people don't get a problem they'll just put it in everything and then when you apply that to whole populations there are sections of the population who really show negative symptoms and they just think there's something wrong with them whereas actually they're eating foods that you would never in a million years literally have eaten until the last you know hundred years and industrialization of food so it it's really and it's something which I think is um true with a lot of the diets that people find benefit from like even if you just go keto it's actually a lot harder to eat as much processed food cuz even like a sausage I was looking at sausages and how much carbon stuff and other ingredients they end up mixing so if you just eat food that you can that you could pick and name you automatically have a very different diet and you've excluded lots of things because most of the ingredients are industrial they're not really yeah and I think that's well

it made my skin so without really exploring that so much I could start to make a link um another thing is like fructose syrup glucose fructose syrup in chocolates and sweets didn't like it my skin didn't like it and I would rather because I was in that much pain I would rather just not eat it at that point um but yeah that was there that aspect of my microbiome and looking at that was one element um then I saw started to do then I started to explore like private blood tests and because you can get like blood tests for everything these days um and I found out that my vitamin D was low um and I was quite surprised because you don't I mean it's a common thing but when it happens to you you're like oh why have I got low vitamin D um so yeah I found out that there's a link between vitamin D levels in your body

and deficiency and skin issues and there's this um this antimicrobial called cathal sidin which if you have low levels of that because you have vitamin D deficiency you tend to have skin issues so I was like oh there's an actual there's a valid link here and also we know that vitamin D contributes to um I think it's called figrin no it's filigran something like that I'll just ignore that but yeah there's so there's a link um and I actually found that when I supplemented with vitamin D um my flareups cuz I still having some flareups but the gap between flareups became further and further apart so I'm having more periods of remission um which was great at that point but um I then had to like supplement with vitamin D and then you kind of think about okay what level of vitamin D we're told in the NHS that um you have 400 to

600 units and that's kind of a maintenance vitamin D supplement um but having been following people like hberman and Dr Ronda Patrick they keep their levels of vitamin D between 40 to 60 nanograms per mil and mine was below that so um they actually maintain their vitamin D levels higher than what they State the guideline state to say to maintain it one of the interesting things about guidelines the patient I don't think many patients know is that the average when they say what the normal range is that is just defined by a group of normal people and so if you're normal population is a bit deficient in vitamin D that you actually tell everyone that deficient is normal I'm not saying that everyone who's deficient that his normal is deficient but there is to be taken into account that if you're close to the lower end of normal probably if we compared you with people from 100 years ago outside all the time you would be well in the deficient range so it's a useful idea not to in certain areas not to

think about the normal range as something to aim for well um there was an interesting study it was a peer review in the uh Journal of what is it in the journal I was going to say like steroid chemistry and molecular biology um and it looked at over a seven-year period it looked at um patients taking between 5,000 to 50,000 units of vitamin D over 7even years and they didn't have any issues and that's really interesting because with our maintenance is 400 to 600 as a guideline state but actually when you go outside your body makes 25,000 units of vitamin D so there's a bit of a discrepancy um and the reason why I'm going down this is because I realized the vitamin D once I up the vitamin D levels my eczema

started to improve I had a longer periods of remission um but this study kind of solidified that to me that um we can tolerate a lot more vitamin D but obviously there is that issue of hypocalcemia which is with high levels of vitamin D you're going to have um bone mobilize calcium mobilization from bone but interestingly none of those that cohort that they dosed with over seven years they didn't have any problems so yeah I think there's something to be said for research like that and especially if you're exploring vitamin D can be so useful you know it's worth exploring MH and then there a couple of things about vitamin D as well which I've discovered with patients is if you basically if you have a malabsorption syndrome you don't absorb it anywhere way so Celiac patients even if they take a supplement are often deficient um even years afterwards cuz sometimes your gut takes

a while to recover and you should take it with fat as well so a lot of people uh take a dry powder form but if you take it with a black coffee you don't absorb it so it needs to be with fat and you should probably take it in the morning because otherwise you don't sleep as well because you get your calian rhythms obviously associate high vitamin D with sunlight so if you take it right before bed that's not always good depresses melatonin um and you should also take it uh with K2 vitamin K2 to avoid the calcium going to your arteries instead I can't remember the mechanism but it's just little things you pick up because it's very easy to take a vitamin for years and actually make several mistakes yeah um without knowing a bit more about it so anyone listening to this I would think about those things too yeah definitely so where are we on the journey so you've now switched the you you've altered your the acidity of your stomach which made a difference you picked up this correlation with processed foods and Seed oils in particular um and vitamin D

supplementation at a higher rate MH what did you do next there were other little things that I did along the way so um during this period was almost like a year and a half of self experimentation I did not touch any creams any steroid creams um steroid creams we use them we prescribe them in clinic but they aren't so great for us skin they disrupt the stratum canum um degranulate the skin cause like they're just basically they're not really helpful so emolient are some that something that we push as well to interrupt this the itch scratch cycle and I started using like stopped using any kind of skincare products anything to wash my face with I was I even stopped washing my face with hot water so I didn't want to dry out My Skin Barrier anymore I wanted to support

it um so there were little things like just washing with cold water and avoiding any skin products unless it was aolan um [Music] and also looking at the skin itself you know you're told to drink loads of water because it'll be really good for your skin you know it will hydrate skin but actually you need to have the Skin Barrier itself needs to be able to hold that water so looking at omega-3 supplementation to build back a lipid layer the lipid layer of your skin and having cholesterol like Foods fatty foods basically that would um or foods high in omega-3 that would help support the actual Skin Barrier itself um so that when I drank a lot of water my skin could actually hold on to it and it improved the quality of my skin um and that was an issue for me because I've been putting steroid cream on and like you get skin thinning and I had discoloration of my skin and I just felt

like it wasn't it was a bit weak my skin just didn't seem to go back to what it was before it took a bit of time so I wanted to support my skin in that way so how long has it been since you had a flare up and are there still things that you would that would click you back into it or do you think or have you dabbled with a bit of processed food and not had the same experience like where is it do you feel like it's a chronic thing that you will have to always make the right decision around or is it something that you can cure essentially I would say my body sus susceptible to eczema but I would say that I'm now empowered to manage it myself I haven't had a flare up in about don't remember the last time was December 2022 where it was right after Christmas and I was eating just loads of junk food over Christmas um lots of chocolate and stuff and then I had a flare up and I'm like

whoa I haven't had this in a while so then you just Implement what you know um you'd basically avoid anything like highly processed foods and my skin returned back to normal I don't remember since then having anything um no proper flare it's nothing like what I used to suffer with nothing like that at all um I do however I think I went to D um a couple of months ago and I had like a dish and it was full of oil and I knew it wasn't it was going to be that like processed type oil and lo and behold my like eyes I usually get the indicator sign and my eyes just feel a little bit like they look a bit papery my eyelids that is my sign and that's my sign that my body's telling me okay you know I don't like this and I just pick up and read the signs on my skin almost before it gets any anything into anything worse than that but I'd say my body is bounced back from that my Skin's bounced

back and the stuff that used to bother me doesn't affect me like I'm not vegan anymore and you know I'm not um perfect I'll have like ultr processed foods you go to like KFC or McDonald's and it doesn't affect me in the same way that it did but I can't go back to eating that like every week I just can't because my skin will start to my body will start to regress and go back to that and if I have antibiotics chances are I'm going to really throw it back um if I let my vitamin D levels go return to low levels I'll throw it off even more and I could I reckon I could reverse engineer it and go back to eczema but that's the kind of power that you have now either eat the cake or you fast or you know you'd make the choices that you know will Empower you when I look at everything you've shared with us there's nothing there that isn't good for you generally and so it feel makes me feel very confident that we should be saying you know try this stuff yeah by all means see your GP and do the steroid

stuff if you need to as well but also remove processed food get your vitamin D checked increase your fiber intake you know all the other things that help you protect your microbiome don't take antibiotics unnecessarily or too aggressively these things are all fantastic advice generally so we need to frame it within that Pro Health way of seeing things because that also makes people feel better when they do it there's a lot of resistance around doing things because you have to take on the idenity as a sick of a sick person so when you put on your steroid cream you're taking on the idenity as a sick person whereas actually when you eat more healthily you're taking on the identity of someone who's in control of their health and I think that's the psychological addiction that we want people to be addicted to the right things not the feeling of tasty food but the feeling of being in control of your health and I think that's what you capture it's the mindset shift between putting a plaster on something going down to the root cause of something it's just that shift yeah excellent thank you
very much Alicia
